SOL-3 / TERRESTRIAL / OBSERVED FROM WITHIN

Instruments for
a habitable planet.

Terra Laboratories builds software, databases, apps, and in-situ tools for environmental work. We treat Earth like the first exoplanet we will ever truly characterize, and we ship the instruments to do it.

001 / Thesis

Earth, classified
as an exoplanet.

WORKING HYPOTHESIS
// LONG HORIZON
UPDATED 2026.05
OBSERVATORY PLATE / ENTRY 001

If Earth were an
exoplanet, what would
we want to know about it?

Imagine our species first reached this rock tomorrow. We would build the best instruments we could to study it. Sensors in the water, networks across the air, satellites watching the surface, databases knitting it all together. We would treat every reef, river, and ridge as a frontier.

We already live here, so we don't. Terra Laboratories thinks that's the mistake. The tools we build for Earth today are the same tools humanity needs at every world we visit next.

002 / Products

Instruments
we ship.

7 ACTIVE MODULES
// 3 LINES / 1 PLATFORM
// HARDWARE / BN-04 LIVE / 47 DEPLOYED

Buoy·Node

Solar-powered in-situ monitoring

Temperature, salinity, turbidity, dissolved oxygen, current. Streams over cellular and Iridium. Built to survive ninety-knot weather and a curious sea lion.

IoTLoRaWANIridiumSolarIP68
Spec sheet
// PLATFORM / FOS LIVE / v2.1

FieldOS

The core platform

A unified workspace where field teams ingest sensor data, query satellite layers, run analyses, manage compliance, and ship reports. Backed by an open queryable database.

WebMulti-tenantAPI-firstPostGISS3
Open platform
// MOBILE / FB LIVE / iOS · ANDROID

FieldBook

The field app

The field app that does not betray you when you lose signal. Offline-first data entry, GPS-tagged photos, voice notes, custom forms.

iOSAndroidOffline
Download
// DATABASE / TDB LIVE

TerraDB

The planetary data layer

Public dataset mirrors, team-private streams, satellite imagery, species occurrence. One queryable Postgres + PostGIS warehouse.

PostgresPostGISSTACGraphQL
Read docs
// HARDWARE / DC BETA

DriftCam

Subsurface ML camera

Compact subsurface camera array with onboard ML inference. Counts kelp canopy, IDs species, flags anomalies at the node.

Edge AIJetsonSubsea
Request beta
// RESEARCH / AST RESEARCH / LONG HORIZON

Astra — adapting the stack for off-world bodies.

Our long-horizon track. The same primitives (sense, ingest, analyze, ship) applied to atmospheres that are not Earth's. Currently research, not product. We are building it so that when someone needs it, the tools already exist.

Read the paper
003 / Platform

One workspace.
Every signal on
your coastline.

FIELDOS / v2.1
// REAL-TIME / OPEN API

The dashboard your field team actually opens.

Live telemetry from every node. Time-series across stations. Asset maps with real bathymetry. Anomaly alerts that fire before the spreadsheet would have caught them.

01
Live ingest from every asset Buoys, gliders, fixed stations, AUVs. One pane.
02
Anomaly detection on every stream Models tuned per deployment. Alerts that mean something.
03
Open API beneath everything Pull your own data into your own notebook. We do not gate it.
OCEAN SENTINEL / LIVE
004 / Stack

From sensor
to signal.

4 STAGES / 1 PIPELINE
// OPEN AT EVERY LAYER
→ 01 / SENSE

In-situ capture.

Buoy nodes, DriftCams, and the FieldBook app collect environmental data wherever the work is, however unforgiving the conditions.

→ 02 / INGEST

Stream and store.

TerraDB ingests sensor streams, field uploads, and satellite layers into one queryable warehouse. Open at every layer.

→ 03 / ANALYZE

Model and detect.

Built-in models, anomaly detection, and a full notebook environment for your own analyses on top of your own data.

→ 04 / SHIP

Reports and decisions.

Compliance reports, monitoring summaries, and stakeholder dashboards. Generated automatically. Edited by hand.

// FROM THE FIELD
The instruments built for a kelp forest on Earth are the same instruments built for an ocean under ice on Europa. Planetary observation is one problem at every scale.
FOUNDING NOTE / TERRA LABS / 2024.06
005 / In the Field

Sensors in
the water.

3 COASTLINES / 12+ ORGS
// SINCE 2024
DEPLOYMENT / POINT REYES / 2026.04

The field teaches
the product.
The product earns its
place back in the field.

FieldOS runs across kelp restoration projects, marine protected areas, harbor monitoring programs, and coastal development sites. Each deployment is a feedback loop.

This is not a demo company. Our buoys have been on station for over 18 months. Our software has been in the hands of people who would rather use Excel, and we earned it.

Buoy nodes deployed

Active workspaces

006 / Horizons

The roadmap.

EARTH → ORBIT → BEYOND
// THREE HORIZONS
// HORIZON I / 2024-2027NOW

Earth
instruments.

The foundation. Sensors, software, and database for environmental teams working coastlines today. Everything we ship right now lives here.

  • FieldOS v2 → v3
  • Buoy·Node fleet → 200 units
  • TerraDB public release
  • FieldBook v1 GA
// HORIZON II / 2027-2030NEXT

Planetary
monitoring.

Scale up. From local coastlines to global signals. Satellite ingestion at scale, distributed sensor swarms, anomaly detection across entire ocean basins.

  • Constellation-grade satellite ingest
  • Swarm autonomy for Buoy·Node
  • CoastSight / GA
  • Carbon-flux instruments / alpha
// HORIZON III / 2030+BEYOND

Off-world
application.

The long bet. The same instruments and stack, redesigned for atmospheres and bodies that are not Earth. Currently research, not product.

  • Astra / adaptation studies
  • Cryo-environment sensor R&D
  • Partner missions / TBD
  • Open spec / planetary stack
007 / Trajectory

The long arc.

FROM 2024 / TO 2100+
// MILESTONES & INTENT

A company is a bet on time. Terra Laboratories is a hundred-year company. The instruments we are building today are stepping stones to a single long-horizon outcome: a permanent, open, planetary observation infrastructure that operates across worlds. Here is how we get there.

2024// Origin

Founded in San Diego.

Terra Laboratories begins as a small team building software for a single kelp restoration project. First version of FieldOS deployed on a working coastline within ninety days of incorporation.

Archive / Q2 2024
2025// First fleet

First Buoy·Node fleet in the water.

Hardware program launches. Initial twelve nodes deployed across three Marine Protected Areas. First continuous year of on-station environmental telemetry shipped through FieldOS.

Archive / 2025
2026// Present

Where we are now.

47 nodes online. 23 active workspaces. FieldOS v2.1 in production. TerraDB in private beta with research partners. First 18-month uptime milestone passed for the original fleet. This is the foundation everything else is built on.

Live / Q2 2026
2027// Horizon I

FieldOS public release. 200-node fleet.

The platform becomes self-serve for any environmental program with a coastline. TerraDB opens to the public with mirrors of every major open environmental dataset. We stop being a project and start being infrastructure.

Roadmap / 2027
2030// Horizon II

Satellite ingest at constellation scale. Swarm autonomy.

From local coastlines to global signals. Anomaly detection across entire ocean basins. Carbon-flux instruments in alpha. The first hundred-node coordinated deployment runs without human intervention for a full season.

Roadmap / 2030
2035// Horizon II+

The first thousand-node planetary mesh.

An interoperable network of Terra-spec instruments across every continent and ocean basin, operated by hundreds of partner organizations. Open data layers feed climate science, fisheries, restoration, and policy at planetary scale.

Target / 2035
2040// Horizon III

First off-world deployment of a Terra-spec sensor.

Astra graduates from research to flight hardware. A Terra Laboratories instrument flies as a hosted payload on a partner mission to lunar orbit or Mars surface. The same primitives that monitor a kelp forest measure a place that has never been measured before.

Aspiration / 2040
2050// Horizon III

An open instrumentation standard, adopted everywhere.

The Terra protocol becomes what TCP/IP became for the internet: a permissionless, open layer that anyone can build on. Every environmental sensor on Earth, and the first ones off it, speak the same language. We do not have to own the network. We just have to make it exist.

Aspiration / 2050
2070// Horizon IV

Planetary observation, multi-world.

Habitable-zone targets get the same instrumentation rigor we are now applying to Earth's coastlines. A Terra-derived platform supports the first continuous, long-baseline science campaigns on Europa, Enceladus, or the surface of a candidate exoplanet via swarm probes.

Aspiration / 2070
2100+// The long bet

Infrastructure that outlives its founders.

A planetary instrumentation layer maintained by institutions older than any single company, applied to Earth's biosphere and to every world humanity reaches. The work is no longer ours. It is everyone's. That is the success condition.

The long bet
Mission, stated plainly

Build the instruments that let us understand the only biosphere we have proof of, then take those instruments to every world we can reach.

Most companies are organized around the next quarter. A few are organized around the next decade. Almost none are organized around the next century. We are trying to be in the last group.

The reason is simple. Planetary observation is a multi-generation problem. The instruments we install in 2026 will be replaced four times before their measurements stop mattering. The standards we set in 2030 will be the foundation other people build on for the rest of this century. The data we capture today is what someone in 2150 will use to know what was already lost, and what was still here to save.

That is the work. That is the bet. We are building it like it has to last.

// GET IN TOUCH

Building for Earth
today. For the
stars tomorrow.

If you run an environmental program, lead a research team, or want to bring better instruments to your coastline, we want to hear from you.
